content.text: GenAI integration code accumulates complexity faster than most software because its components — prompts, model calls, retry logic, output parsing, and evaluation harnesses — are frequently modified together but should be changed independently. Disciplined refactoring applies the same layering and separation principles that make traditional software maintainable to these AI-specific concerns.
What it is

Refactoring discipline for agentic AI integration code is the practice of restructuring LLM call sites, prompt management, output parsing, and retry logic into well-defined, independently testable layers without changing observable system behavior. It borrows Martin Fowler's definition of refactoring — improving internal structure without altering external behavior — and extends it to cover the characteristics unique to AI integration: non-deterministic outputs, cost-per-call economics, prompt drift over time, and the need to swap underlying models without touching business logic.

The core concern is that AI integration code written quickly tends to entangle concerns that must later be separated. A common anti-pattern is a 400-line function that fetches user context, builds a prompt string with f-string interpolation, calls the LLM API, parses the JSON response with ad-hoc regex, retries on failures with hard-coded backoff, and writes the result to a database — all in one block. Refactoring this code means introducing explicit boundaries so each of those concerns can be modified, tested, and deployed independently.

The refactoring targets are predictable: LLM call sites that mix prompt construction with response parsing; retry logic duplicated across multiple call sites; hard-coded model identifiers scattered through the codebase; token count estimation embedded in business logic; and cost accounting hidden inside API wrappers. Identifying and extracting these is the daily discipline of AI integration refactoring.
Why it matters

AI integration code that lacks clear layer boundaries becomes progressively harder to change. Switching from one model provider to another requires touching dozens of files. Adding exponential backoff to retry logic requires finding every retry loop. Updating a system prompt requires understanding all the places where prompt fragments are assembled. Each of these changes carries regression risk proportional to the entanglement.

Refactoring AI integration code also directly affects the quality and reliability of the evaluation harness. If prompt construction is tangled with business logic, it is impossible to write an eval test that exercises the prompt in isolation from the business logic. If response parsing is embedded in the call site, testing the parser requires mocking the entire LLM call. Clean layer boundaries are a prerequisite for a useful eval suite.

Beyond testability, well-refactored AI integration code is cheaper to operate. Extracting cost tracking into a dedicated layer makes it straightforward to add per-request budget enforcement. Centralizing retry logic makes it easy to tune backoff parameters globally rather than hunt for every instance. These operational improvements compound over the lifetime of the system.
Benefits
Model portability: swapping the underlying LLM requires changes in one adapter class, not throughout the business logic.
Testable prompt layer: prompt construction can be tested against expected templates without making real LLM API calls.
Isolated retry tuning: changing backoff parameters, jitter, and retry budgets in one place affects all call sites.
Accurate cost tracking: a centralized gateway can instrument every LLM call with token counts and cost metadata without modifying call sites.
Reduced merge conflict risk: prompts, business logic, and model configuration live in separate files and change on different cadences, reducing concurrent-edit conflicts.
Eval harness reuse: standardized response types from the gateway layer make it trivial to route the same inputs through the eval harness and production code.
Trade-offs
Upfront refactoring cost: introducing a gateway layer and adapter pattern requires significant initial investment if the codebase is already large and entangled.
Abstraction overhead: the gateway interface must be kept in sync with the capabilities of the underlying model; new model features (streaming, multimodal inputs) require updating the interface.
Risk during refactoring: even behavior-preserving refactors carry regression risk for non-deterministic systems; the eval suite must be run before and after every significant refactor.
Over-abstraction temptation: teams may introduce unnecessary abstraction layers that add complexity without adding flexibility; prefer the simplest boundary that enables the required tests.
How it works

The layering model for AI integration code has four strata. The business logic layer contains domain objects, orchestration, and decision rules. It calls the LLM gateway through an interface and has no knowledge of which model is in use. The LLM gateway layer implements the interface and handles prompt construction, response parsing, streaming, and serialization. The retry and resilience layer wraps gateway calls with backoff, jitter, timeout enforcement, and circuit breaking. The model adapter layer is the thin translation layer between the gateway's canonical request format and the wire format required by a specific model provider's SDK.

This separation means that a change to the system prompt lives exclusively in the gateway layer. A change to retry parameters lives in the resilience layer. A change to the model provider (e.g., from Anthropic Claude to a self-hosted model) requires only a new model adapter implementation; the gateway, resilience, and business logic layers are unchanged.

The gateway interface should define canonical request and response types. The request type carries the prompt content, temperature, max tokens, and any structured input data. The response type carries the parsed output as a typed object, the raw model response for debugging, and metadata including token counts and latency. Business logic receives only the typed output object; it never parses model-specific response formats.
Architecture

attrs.alt: GenAI integration code refactoring: tangled before vs layered after showing Business Logic, LLM Gateway, Model Adapter, and Eval Harness layers

content.text: The left side of the diagram shows the common anti-pattern: business logic, LLM calls, retry logic, prompt construction, and response parsing all in one block, with hard-coded model IDs scattered throughout. The right side shows the target architecture after refactoring: a clear stack from Business Logic through LLM Gateway to Model Adapter, with an Eval Harness that can exercise each layer independently.
When to use it

Apply refactoring discipline immediately when any of the following conditions appear in the codebase: LLM call sites contain inline prompt string construction; retry logic is duplicated at more than two call sites; the model identifier string appears in more than one file; response parsing (JSON extraction, regex parsing, output validation) is co-located with business logic; or adding a new model provider would require touching more than two files.

Schedule a dedicated refactoring sprint when the team is preparing to switch model providers, upgrade to a new model version with changed output formats, or expand to multi-model routing. These milestones create natural forcing functions for the refactoring work that must happen anyway.

Do not refactor prematurely. A prototype or MVP with a single call site, one model, and no eval suite does not benefit from the full gateway-adapter pattern. Introduce abstraction at the point where a specific test, migration, or operational requirement demands it.
How to implement
Map the existing call sites: grep for LLM API calls, list every file that constructs prompts or parses responses, and sketch the current flow.
Define canonical request and response types: create dataclasses or Pydantic models for LLMRequest and LLMResponse before touching any existing code.

Extract a gateway class: move all prompt construction, response parsing, and serialization into one class that implements an LLMGateway interface; keep the existing call site logic but delegate to the gateway.
Extract a model adapter: create one adapter class per model provider that translates LLMRequest to the provider's SDK format and translates the provider's response to LLMResponse.
Centralize retry logic: wrap the model adapter call in a resilience decorator or wrapper class that handles backoff, jitter, and timeouts; remove any per-call-site retry loops.
Run the eval suite before and after each extraction step to confirm behavioral equivalence; do not proceed to the next step if any eval regressions appear.
Examples
Before: tangled call site
After: layered implementation
Real-world example

A team building a customer support ticket classification agent had its LLM call site inline in the Django view handler. When Anthropic released claude-haiku-4-5 and the team wanted to route low-priority tickets through the cheaper model while keeping high-priority tickets on claude-sonnet-4-5, the change required touching 14 files. The work took two engineers four days and introduced two regressions caught by the eval suite.

After a refactoring sprint that introduced the gateway-adapter pattern, model routing became a configuration change in the adapter factory. The same use case was subsequently re-implemented in 45 minutes by one engineer with no regressions. The refactoring also enabled the team to write gateway-level unit tests that run against a mock adapter in 200ms, replacing integration tests that previously required 45 seconds per run.
Common mistakes
Extracting the class but not the type boundary
Returning raw SDK types from the gateway defeats the purpose of the abstraction. The business logic becomes coupled to the SDK anyway, and swapping models still requires touching the caller.
Putting retry logic inside the gateway

Retry logic belongs in a wrapper outside the gateway, not inside the gateway implementation. If retry logic is inside the gateway, it cannot be disabled in tests, cannot be tuned globally, and cannot be composed with circuit breakers or bulkheads. Separate the concerns: the gateway constructs and parses; the retry wrapper controls execution.
Best practices
Define canonical LLMRequest and LLMResponse types before writing any gateway code; they are the contract between layers.
Keep prompt templates in version-controlled string constants or files, never inline in the call site as f-strings.
Test the parsing layer (gateway._parse) with fixed raw response strings; never write parser tests that require a live model call.
Use a dependency injection container or factory to wire adapters, so changing the model in all environments is a configuration change, not a code change.
Track input and output token counts in LLMResponse; the business logic should never need to call tiktoken or equivalent on its own.
Treat the gateway interface as a public API: add new parameters only additively, and version the interface if a breaking change is unavoidable.
Performance and scalability

The layering pattern adds a small amount of object allocation overhead per request, typically one to three microseconds for dataclass instantiation. This is negligible relative to the 50 to 3000 millisecond latency of the LLM API call itself and should never be a reason to skip the abstraction.

Centralizing retry logic in a single wrapper enables tuning based on production latency data. A circuit breaker pattern applied at the adapter level prevents a degraded model API from cascading into the business logic layer, allowing the circuit to open after a configurable failure threshold and recover gracefully. Without centralized retry and circuit breaking, each call site degrades independently and monitoring becomes difficult.
Tooling and ecosystem
LangChain / LlamaIndex: provide pre-built gateway-style abstractions (LLM wrappers, output parsers) that follow similar layering principles; useful for teams that prefer to use an existing framework.
Pydantic: the canonical choice for defining typed request and response objects; its validation layer can enforce output schema compliance before returning from the gateway.
Tenacity: a Python retry library that provides configurable backoff, jitter, and retry condition decorators; well-suited for the retry wrapper layer.
pytest with httpx mock: enables testing the gateway layer with mocked HTTP responses, bypassing the real model API without requiring a separate mock server.
Comparison with alternatives

Partial

Medium

Low

Security and reliability

The gateway layer is the correct place to enforce prompt injection defenses. Input sanitization that strips adversarial instruction sequences from user-supplied data should happen in the gateway before prompt construction, not in the business logic layer. Centralizing sanitization prevents injection vectors from being missed when new call sites are added.

For reliability, the adapter should enforce a maximum token budget per request and raise a typed exception if the response is truncated (stop_reason == 'max_tokens'). The gateway should map this condition to a typed error that the business logic can handle explicitly — for example, by requesting a summarized input and retrying — rather than silently returning a truncated response.
Key takeaways
AI integration code that mixes prompt construction, model calls, retry logic, and response parsing in one block becomes expensive to change and impossible to test in isolation.
The four-layer pattern — Business Logic, LLM Gateway, Retry Wrapper, Model Adapter — separates concerns so each can be modified and tested independently.
Canonical request and response types are the contract between layers; the business logic should never receive raw SDK types or parse model-specific response formats.
Retry logic belongs in a wrapper outside the gateway, not inside it; this enables global tuning, test disabling, and composition with circuit breakers.
Run the eval suite before and after every refactoring step to confirm behavioral equivalence in a non-deterministic system.
The abstraction pays for itself the first time the team needs to swap a model provider, tune retry parameters globally, or add centralized cost tracking.
